category_summary:
The dominant story was an apparent implosion at **Alibaba's Qwen** team. **Jeremy Howard** [raised the alarm](/?date=2026-03-05&category=social#item-95ec59202914) about a mass exodus of top researchers immediately after the acclaimed **Qwen 3.5** open-weight release, and **Simon Willison** published detailed notes connecting the departures to organizational upheaval. At least one departing researcher appears to have joined **Anthropic**.

- **Anthropic** faced a turbulent geopolitical week: despite the **Pentagon** banning **Claude**, it [reportedly still powers](/?date=2026-03-05&category=social#item-1db4bd7076eb) critical U.S. military operations in Iran. CEO **Dario Amodei** [fired back in an internal memo](/?date=2026-03-05&category=social#item-2909d3ec7794), calling **OpenAI's** Pentagon deal "safety theater" and alleging political retaliation.
- **Donald Knuth** published a paper praising **Claude**, a remarkable endorsement from one of computing's most important figures.
- **Jensen Huang** [declared **OpenClaw**](/?date=2026-03-05&category=social#item-35aedec89b15) the most important software release ever, claiming it surpassed **Linux** in adoption speed.
- **OpenAI** is reportedly [adding an "extreme" reasoning mode](/?date=2026-03-05&category=social#item-6961b032d1a4) to **GPT-5.4** with a 1M context window, per **The Information**.
- **Ethan Mollick** offered two striking insights: [predicting frontier open-weight models](/?date=2026-03-05&category=social#item-cdae71a308d2) may cease to exist, and [comparing pre-2022 content](/?date=2026-03-05&category=social#item-60631d00407d) to rare uncontaminated materials like Roman shipwreck lead—free from AI-generated noise.

items.title:
A trillion-parameter model just made half its brain disappear. It got smarter.

Yuan3.0 Ultra is a n...
items.content:
A trillion-parameter model just made half its brain disappear. It got smarter.

Yuan3.0 Ultra is a new open-source multimodal MoE model from Yuan Lab. 1010B total parameters, only 68.8B active at inference.

It beat GPT-5.2, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and Claude Opus 4.6 on RAG benchmarks by wide margins. 

67.4% on Docmatix vs GPT-4o's 56.8%.

Here's what it unlocks:
> Enterprise RAG with 68.2% avg accuracy across 10 retrieval tasks
> Complex table understanding at 62.3% on MMTab
> Text-to-SQL generation scoring 83.9% on Spider 1.0
> Multimodal doc analysis with a 64K context window

The key innovation: Layer-Adaptive Expert Pruning (LAEP).

During pretraining, expert token loads become wildly imbalanced. Some experts get 500x more tokens than others. 

LAEP prunes the underused ones layer by layer, cutting 33% of parameters while boosting training efficiency by 49%.

They also refined "fast-thinking" RL. Correct answers with fewer reasoning steps get rewarded more. This cut output tokens by 14.38% while improving accuracy by 16.33%.

The bigger signal here: MoE models are learning to self-compress during training, not after. If pruning becomes part of pretraining, the cost curve for trillion-scale models shifts dramatically.

items.title: We need to transition the conversation from Claude being the first company to go all in on code to h...
items.content:
We need to transition the conversation from Claude being the first company to go all in on code to how they clearly were way ahead on general agent behavior. 

Could be a bigger deal, as I suspect all the labs will “solve” coding. Not sure what the agent secret sauce is.

items.title:
Start building for an agent-first world.

If you have a product, you need to start offering skills f...
items.content:
Start building for an agent-first world.

If you have a product, you need to start offering skills for Claude, Codex, Cursor, and any other agents.

Your skills should specify:

• How to navigate and use your product
• Best practices the agent must follow
• Detailed instructions on how to accomplish things
• Anti-patterns to avoid

Redis is one of the most popular in-memory data stores in the world, and they just released their agent skills.

It takes one second to install, and it will turn your agent into a Senior Redis Engineer:

$ npx skills add redis/agent-skills

In the attached video, I show you how to install it as a plugin in Claude Code and some of its benefits.

This is the easiest way to "teach" models what they don't know and keep their knowledge up to date.

If you ask me, skills is literally one of the most brilliant ideas that Anthropic has put out there.

If you use Redis, their skill is a must-have. If you don't, this skill will show you how to build and structure yours.

Here is what their skill teaches your agent:

1. Current patterns for common use cases: caching, rate limiting, session management, vector search, semantic caching, pub/sub, streams.

2. Which data structure to use and when: hashes vs. JSON vs. sorted sets vs. vector sets.

3. Anti-patterns to avoid: no KEYS in loops, no unbounded key growth, no large values that amplify every operation.

4. Production-aware defaults: connection pooling, pipelining, cluster compatibility, error handling that doesn't silently swallow failures.

items.title:
🚨 You can now run AI agents 24/7 for less than lunch money.

Google just released Gemini 3.1 Flash-L...
items.content:
🚨 You can now run AI agents 24/7 for less than lunch money.

Google just released Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite. It costs $0.25 per million input tokens and $1.50 per million output tokens.

The model beats its predecessor on speed and reasoning.

It delivers 2.5x faster Time to First Answer Token and 45% increase in output speed compared to Gemini 2.5 Flash.

On the GPQA Diamond benchmark, it scored 86.9%, matching larger models.

The standout feature is adjustable thinking levels.

You can control how much reasoning the model performs by choosing from minimal, low, medium, or high thinking levels.

This lets you balance speed and quality based on task complexity.

What this unlocks:

> High volume translation and content moderation
> UI and dashboard generation at scale
> AI agent routing and classification
> Real-time chat with complex instruction following

Best for high-volume agentic tasks, simple data extraction, and extremely low-latency applications where budget and speed are the primary constraints.

https://t.co/3mgciQaNJW
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI covers Google's Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite release: $0.25/M input tokens, $1.50/M output, 2.5x faster TTFAT, 45% faster output than Gemini 2.5 Flash, 86.9% on GPQA Diamond. Features adjustable thinking levels (minimal to high).
items.reasoning: [Staleness check: primary subject released 106 days ago (GA: 2025-11-18), score capped] Covers a just-released Google model (GA: 2026-03-03, one day before coverage date). The extremely low pricing and adjustable thinking levels are significant for the agentic AI cost curve. Aligns with the trend of cheap inference models.

An existential risk for near term open-weight models. In the coming years, the only places with business reasons for building them

items.title: Model "shallowness" is a big deal in the time of AI agents, models can be very good in narrow areas ...
items.content:
Model "shallowness" is a big deal in the time of AI agents, models can be very good in narrow areas but since they are shallow, they lack context and reasoning to make good judgement calls when doing tasks. 

Once you are operating independently, being good at coding isn't enough
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Emollick discusses 'model shallowness' as a critical issue for AI agents - models can excel in narrow areas but lack the contextual depth and judgment needed for independent task execution. Being good at coding alone isn't sufficient for agents.
items.reasoning: Important conceptual insight about AI agent limitations from a highly credible source. 'Shallowness' is a useful framing for understanding why narrow capabilities don't translate to reliable autonomous agents. Good engagement (12K views).

items.title: It gets at the heart of what current models can't do well: plan an overall process with complicated ...
items.content: It gets at the heart of what current models can't do well: plan an overall process with complicated details and then iterate within each piece, stress testing the whole to see if it holds up as you do so. The models end up committing to a path, and back-justifying their choices.
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Emollick identifies a core limitation of current AI models: inability to plan complex processes, iterate within components, and stress-test holistic solutions. Models commit to paths and back-justify choices.
items.reasoning: Substantive insight from a leading AI commentator about fundamental model limitations in planning and iterative reasoning. Good engagement and highly relevant to understanding current AI capabilities.

The models end up committing to a path, and back-justifying their choices.

I exported my 2025 tax return from TurboTax, blacked out my personal i...
items.content:
Claude just found me $2,500.

I exported my 2025 tax return from TurboTax, blacked out my personal information, and asked Claude to check it for mistakes.

It turns out, I wasn't reporting a backdoor Roth IRA contribution correctly.

I then asked it to check my 2024 return, and Claude confirmed the mistake.

I just filed out an amended return for 2024.
The IRS owes me $2,562.

items.title:
Every single Claude Code conversation turn should end with it offering to do even more for you. 

It...
items.content:
Every single Claude Code conversation turn should end with it offering to do even more for you. 

It should be so proactive and helpful that you cry with joy at how productive it is making you.

Add this to your global https://t.co/6lkRloGo1l file ⬇️

---------------------------------------------------

# Task Endings - "What Else Can I Handle?"

After completing any big task, end with a "Let me take more off your plate" section with three categories:

1. Next actions I can do right now — specific follow-ups I can knock out immediately
2. Automations or systems I can set up — so you never have to do it manually again
3. Things to delegate to your team — draft messages for <insert team members>

3-5 bullet points max, no fluff, goal is you walk away feeling lighter.

items.title: On ForecastBench, our AI forecasting benchmark, superforecasters achieve a Brier Index of 70.6%. The...
items.content:
On ForecastBench, our AI forecasting benchmark, superforecasters achieve a Brier Index of 70.6%. The best LLMs—CassiAI’s ensemble_2_crowdadj and xAI’s Grok 4.20 (Preview)—score 67.9%, a 2.7 percentage point (pp) gap.

For context: state-of-the-art LLM performance improved by 2.4 pp from Claude 3.5 Sonnet (Oct 2024) to Grok 4.20 (Preview, Oct 2025) over one year. So, the current gap represents roughly one year of LLM progress.
items.author: @Research_FRI

items.reasoning: Quantitative benchmark data showing LLM forecasting closing in on superforecasters. Notable mention of Grok 4.20 Preview performance. Concrete metrics on AI capability progression.

items.title: Open weights frontier models are incredibly expensive to make, and, unlike open source models, there...
items.content: Open weights frontier models are incredibly expensive to make, and, unlike open source models, there is no path to recoup that cost. You can’t easily sell ancillary services or complementary assets once the weights are out there, unlike open source plays like Red Hat or Android.

items.title: Extrapolating linearly, this puts LLM–superforecaster parity around May 2027 (95% CI: April 2026 – M...
items.content:
Extrapolating linearly, this puts LLM–superforecaster parity around May 2027 (95% CI: April 2026 – May 2028). That’s a few months later than our previous Brier-score-based estimate of March 2027.

The shift is a modeling artifact: the Brier Index is a convex transformation, so the same Brier score improvement translates into progressively larger Brier Index gains at high accuracy. A linear trend in Brier score looks like an accelerating trend in Brier Index.

items.title: New course: Build and Train an LLM with JAX, built in partnership with @Google and taught by @chrisa...
items.content:
New course: Build and Train an LLM with JAX, built in partnership with @Google and taught by @chrisachard.

JAX is the open-source library behind Google's Gemini, Veo, and other advanced models. This short course teaches you to build and train a 20-million parameter language model from scratch using JAX and its ecosystem of tools.

You'll implement a complete MiniGPT-style architecture from scratch, train it, and chat with your finished model through a graphical interface.

Skills you'll gain:
- Learn JAX's core primitives: automatic differentiation, JIT compilation, and vectorized execution
- Build a MiniGPT-style LLM using Flax/NNX, implementing embedding and transformer blocks
- Load a pretrained MiniGPT model and run inference through a chat interface

Come learn this important software layer for building LLMs!

https://t.co/wm6NZOGIKC

items.title:
A new inference algorithm just cut LLM latency in half. 

Not with better hardware. With smarter sch...
items.content:
A new inference algorithm just cut LLM latency in half. 

Not with better hardware. With smarter scheduling.

Speculative Decoding (SD) already sped up inference by having a small model draft tokens for a large model to verify. 

But drafting and verifying still happen sequentially.

Speculative Speculative Decoding (SSD) runs both in parallel. Up to 2x faster than today's strongest inference engines.

What this unlocks:
> Run Llama-70B at draft-model speeds
> Double thinking time for same compute budget
> Longer reasoning chains without latency penalties
> Paged attention + prefix caching stay fully intact

The trick: instead of waiting for verified tokens, SSD drafts anticipated verification outcomes simultaneously. 

Two streams, one forward pass budget. Benchmarked on Llama-3 (70B/1B) and Qwen3 (32B/0.6B).

If 2x latency gains hold at scale, the "inference wall" blocking long-horizon reasoning gets pushed significantly further out. 

The constraint shifts from speed to strategy.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal explains Speculative Speculative Decoding (SSD) - a new inference algorithm that runs draft and verification in parallel, achieving up to 2x latency reduction. Benchmarked on Llama-3 70B/1B and Qwen3 32B/0.6B.

items.title: The most common reflex when speaking about building creative worlds with AI is to think of something...
items.content: The most common reflex when speaking about building creative worlds with AI is to think of something that resembles a spatial space, a video game, or some fictional fantastical science fiction story. I think that is a failure of imagination. worlds should be thought more in the vein of this video. finding new things that feel rare, surprising, and sometimes niche. What lies between interestingness and newness, maybe something that feels just one degree off from what you already know, enough to make it interesting. a world doesn’t need to be large to be deep

items.title:
“The Ultimate Vibe Coding Prompt” — Is this what coding has devolved into? 

I remember (late 1980’s...
items.content:
“The Ultimate Vibe Coding Prompt” — Is this what coding has devolved into? 

I remember (late 1980’s to 1990) when I wrote the entire Hubble Space Telescope Phase 1 proposal entry, processing, and management system, including data entry and management systems, user interfaces, processing infrastructure, and reporting components—over a period of 3+ years, generating entirely by myself 10’s of thousands lines of code—I spent many hundreds of hours eliciting requirements, feedback, use cases, user testing results, etc. from hundreds of individuals worldwide to complete the design, development, validation, deployment, verification, and ultimate successful multi-year operation of the end-to-end system that won me an NASA Individual Achievement Award in 1990. No amount of prompts could ever tap into the globally distributed real human experience and knowledge pool that made all that possible.

items.summary: Kirk Borne provides a detailed critique of 'vibe coding,' contrasting it with his experience building the Hubble Space Telescope proposal management system over 3+ years, arguing that no amount of prompts could replace the deep human requirements gathering process.

items.title: I recently had the pleasure of joining @nikkei podcast to discuss Sakana AI’s vision, the current la...
items.content:
I recently had the pleasure of joining @nikkei podcast to discuss Sakana AI’s vision, the current landscape of AI development, and the strategic path forward for Japan 🇯🇵

As the AI industry moves incredibly fast, I believe it’s crucial to step back and think about how we build these systems responsibly and effectively. During the conversation, we touched on a few key themes that are central to what we do at Sakana AI:

🇯🇵 Japan’s “Hybrid Strategy”
The optimal path forward isn’t about relying 100% on foreign-made AI, nor is it about closing ourselves off with purely domestic models. The key is a hybrid approach—striking the right balance and combining the best technologies created both at home and abroad.

🏯 Why “Sovereign AI” Matters
Building domestic AI capabilities goes far beyond national security. I strongly believe that every country should aim to develop its own AI technology to protect, preserve, and pass down its unique cultural identity, languages, and traditions.

🌱 Towards Sustainable AI Development
We are still at a stage where we must ask ourselves: Will the massive capital currently being poured into AI truly pay off in the long run from a sustainability standpoint? At Sakana AI, we believe that enormous compute resources aren’t always required to train a new foundation model. Efficiency matters, and there is still immense room for innovation everywhere.

You can read the full article and listen to the podcast episode (in Japanese) here:

Just to be clear here:

I made the original mistake in 2024 (and almost made it in 2025 as well.) Th...
items.content:
Just to be clear here:

I made the original mistake in 2024 (and almost made it in 2025 as well.) This was not a TurboTax mistake.

Claude read the forms and explained what the numbers meant. This explanation made me realize that I was not reporting my backdoor correctly, and therefore, I overpaid in 2024.

I told Claude what I meant to do, and then Claude reported back what I was missing.

This is an example of Claude + Human working to fix something that Human alone (me) screwed up.

items.title: The first day I got my Google Glasses home my wife asked me "can it show me anything about the peopl...
items.content:
The first day I got my Google Glasses home my wife asked me "can it show me anything about the people I'm meeting?"

Meta is planning that on its glasses.

Are we ready for a world where everyone is known? 

I am, but I am admittedly in the post privacy world as a public figure (Grok knows me more deeply than I know myself in many ways). Most everyday people haven't thought about how their privacy is going away.

What will be your reaction if you walk into a store and the clerk there greets you by name because she has one of these glasses on?

Mine will probably be to say hi to her back with her name. 

What a world we are going into. 

There's one place that this will be deeply accepted: events.

I was at a robotics dinner at Silicon Valley last night. Everyone was adding everyone to LinkedIn and X. There this kind of thing would be very helpful, and very accepted. In a couple of weeks at NVIDIA's GTC I wish I had glasses that did this. 

But in everyday life? People will freak out for a while, before it just gets accepted if enough people are using them. 

I can just hear the cries against these coming. A million people freaked out when Zuckerberg first turned on news feeds. Yet we all are using those now.

items.reasoning: Thoughtful, detailed original commentary on a significant AI/privacy topic — facial recognition in Meta glasses. High engagement (11K views, 67 likes). Draws from personal experience and raises important societal questions.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer writes a detailed thread about Meta's planned facial recognition in smart glasses, discussing privacy implications, events use cases, and societal acceptance.
items.content_html:
The first day I got my Google Glasses home my wife asked me "can it show me anything about the people I'm meeting?"

Meta is planning that on its glasses.

Are we ready for a world where everyone is known?

I am, but I am admittedly in the post privacy world as a public figure (Grok knows me more deeply than I know myself in many ways). Most everyday people haven't thought about how their privacy is going away.

What will be your reaction if you walk into a store and the clerk there greets you by name because she has one of these glasses on?

Mine will probably be to say hi to her back with her name.

What a world we are going into.

There's one place that this will be deeply accepted: events.

I was at a robotics dinner at Silicon Valley last night. Everyone was adding everyone to LinkedIn and X. There this kind of thing would be very helpful, and very accepted. In a couple of weeks at NVIDIA's GTC I wish I had glasses that did this.

But in everyday life? People will freak out for a while, before it just gets accepted if enough people are using them.

I can just hear the cries against these coming. A million people freaked out when Zuckerberg first turned on news feeds. Yet we all are using those now.

items.title:
V&V through prompts? I’m not buying it, especially if critical human systems are at stake!

V&V = Va...
items.content:
V&V through prompts? I’m not buying it, especially if critical human systems are at stake!

V&V = Validation & Verification — 2 different things that ask 2 different questions…

“Did I build the system right?”
versus
“Did I build the right system?”

Building to the wrong set of requirements might pass all the unit tests, but fail on critical, but previously unstated (implicit) or unsolicited, human requirements?
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Kirk Borne questions whether Validation & Verification can be done through prompts, especially for critical human systems. Distinguishes between building the system right vs building the right system.
items.reasoning: Substantive and original critique of vibe coding / prompt-based V&V for critical systems. Raises important AI safety and software engineering concerns. From a credible data science and engineering voice.

items.title: I’ve been reading @bgurley's book Runnin' Down a Dream, almost done, and there’s so much value in it...
items.content:
I’ve been reading @bgurley's book Runnin' Down a Dream, almost done, and there’s so much value in it for people trying to find a place to shine in this AI buildout. Lots of people want to build something impactful, but there's not a good playbook for chasing a hunch or gut feeling. It results in a lot of research being in the same area and less diversity (e.g. a ton of RL work right now).

So much of what I do at @interconnectsai is obviously treading my own path, so I resonate greatly, but the book formalizes the ways to push it further and form the right community/network for making the right decisions in it. It makes me think about what the most successful form of it can be in 5 years, or 25.

With AI coming I think the micro-businesses that individuals can create with their personal corner of expertise, values, and personality can be immensely valuable. I recommend reading it and considering how you can lean more into being n of 1.

So much of what I do at @interconnectsai is obviously treading my own path, so I resonate greatly, but the book formalizes the ways to push it further and form the right community/network for making the right decisions in it. items.title: Maybe this is what it sounds like when a model is in the early stages of learning? Or this is what a...
items.content:
Maybe this is what it sounds like when a model is in the early stages of learning? Or this is what a new language might feel like? Idk, but for some obscure reason, I find myself more often than usual thinking of this beautiful piece by Jaap Blonk and @golan.

Ursonography is a project from 2005. It's an audiovisual interpretation of Kurt Schwitters' Ursonate, a masterpiece of 20th-century concrete poetry in which speech is reduced to its most abstract and musical elements. Dutch sound poet and virtuoso vocalist Jaap Blonk has performed the half-hour Ursonate more than a thousand times.

items.title: The Brier score is the standard metric for evaluating forecasting accuracy. But it has a major drawb...
items.content:
The Brier score is the standard metric for evaluating forecasting accuracy. But it has a major drawback: it’s difficult to interpret and communicate.

For example, superforecasters have a Brier score of 0.086 on ForecastBench, our AI forecasting benchmark.

Technically, this means that on average superforecasters are “0.086 squared probability units from the truth.” Confused? You’re not alone.

We propose a simple fix: the Brier Index. 🧵
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI Research introduces the Brier Index as an interpretable alternative to the Brier score for evaluating forecasting accuracy
items.reasoning: Thread starter proposing a new metric standard. Relevant to AI evaluation methodology but niche audience.
items.summary_html: FRI Research introduces the Brier Index as an interpretable alternative to the Brier score for evaluating forecasting accuracy

items.title: "Machine learning is the study of computer algorithms that automatically improve with experience." —...
items.content:
"Machine learning is the study of computer algorithms that automatically improve with experience." — From the first page of Tom Mitchell's book [1997]: https://t.co/d7OkKfkX9R

My paraphrase: "ML is the set of 𝙈𝙖𝙩𝙝𝙚𝙢𝙖𝙩𝙞𝙘𝙖𝙡 𝘼𝙇𝙂𝙊𝙍𝙄𝙏𝙃𝙈𝙎 𝙩𝙝𝙖𝙩 𝙇𝙀𝘼𝙍𝙉 𝙁𝙍𝙊𝙈 𝙀𝙓𝙋𝙀𝙍𝙄𝙀𝙉𝘾𝙀." [𝗺𝘆 𝗲𝗺𝗽𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗶𝘀]

When I read that in 1998, after many years of studying mathematics for physics and astrophysics, it was the first time that I learned about ML, and I asked myself, "𝘞𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘥𝘰𝘦𝘴 𝘵𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘮𝘦𝘢𝘯?" Searching for an answer to that question forever changed the trajectory of my career.
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Kirk Borne shares a personal story about discovering machine learning through Tom Mitchell's 1997 book, reflecting on how ML changed his career trajectory.
items.reasoning: Personal and reflective post from a respected data scientist about his ML journey. Good engagement (70 likes, 3.2K views). Provides historical perspective on ML's evolution.
items.summary_html: Kirk Borne shares a personal story about discovering machine learning through Tom Mitchell's 1997 book, reflecting on how ML changed his career trajectory.
items.content_html:

When I read that in 1998, after many years of studying mathematics for physics and astrophysics, it was the first time that I learned about ML, and I asked myself, "𝘞𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘥𝘰𝘦𝘴 𝘵𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘮𝘦𝘢𝘯?" Searching for an answer to that question forever changed the trajectory of my career.

items.title: @houmanasefi The Ritz Carlton goes through your trash and keeps a database on what you like, like yo...
items.content:
@houmanasefi The Ritz Carlton goes through your trash and keeps a database on what you like, like you ate Cheetos out of the mini bar, to give you better service. And did that 100 years ago. It's a little freaky,  yes, but that's why it is considered the highest service organization now.

At first me knowing someone's name will be a bit freaky. But in 10 years? No it won't be. 

So, the question is how to do this in a way that makes our interactions with each other better and not worse. I don't want to freak out an employee. But I do want to find ways to get better service out of the corporation.

Most of the time when I go to a hotel the staff is wearing a name tag anyway. So name usually isn't it. Maybe showing me the role that person plays and whether he or she can help me get my question answered? I don't care that that employee has two kids at home, that is freaky. But I do care that she can help me figure out how to get access to the pool that's locked, or my room where I lost a key or something like that.

This requires a lot more thinking than I can give in a few seconds here, obviously. And you are right to call it out as a place full of land mines that could lead to some really freaky things.

items.title:
Drug discovery is where this becomes strategic.

Instead of manually stitching databases:

→ ADMET e...
items.content:
Drug discovery is where this becomes strategic.

Instead of manually stitching databases:

→ ADMET evaluation
→ Drug-likeness scoring
→ Repurposing signals via disease pathway and drug-target mapping
→ Multi-omics integration
→ Regulatory network inference and regulon activity scoring

100+ biomedical tools and thousands of recent papers unified into reproducible workflows.

This is how you scale insight across labs and teams.

items.title:
In most labs, the bottleneck is not ideas.

It is orchestration.

→ Single-cell RNA-seq → clustering...
items.content:
In most labs, the bottleneck is not ideas.

It is orchestration.

→ Single-cell RNA-seq → clustering, UMAP visualization, marker annotation
→ Patient phenotypes + variants → ranked causal genes using ClinVar and gnomAD, with transparent reasoning
→ CRISPR screens → structured perturbations, readouts, analysis workflows

Weeks of fragmented scripting become structured execution.

Less glue code.
More science.

Weeks of fragmented scripting become structured execution.

Less glue code.

More science.

What if your next drug discovery pipeline started with one sentence, not 6 months of scripting?

In ...
items.content:
What if your next drug discovery pipeline started with one sentence, not 6 months of scripting?

In this latest deep dive with @scispace, I saw a shift that feels bigger than incremental AI gains.

Upload single-cell data and get clustered cell types, UMAPs, marker annotations, causal gene rankings, and ADMET analysis in one structured flow.

This changes the operating model.

Here’s the breakdown…

What I went through.

items.title:
Brier Index = (1 − √Brier score) × 100%.

The scale:
• 100% = perfect foresight;
• 50% = always pred...
items.content:
Brier Index = (1 − √Brier score) × 100%.

The scale:
• 100% = perfect foresight;
• 50% = always predicting 50%;
• 0% = maximally wrong.

A Brier Index of 70% is the score you’d get by predicting 70% on events that happen and 30% on events that don’t.

The Brier Index is a monotonic transformation of the Brier score. So, it’s still strictly proper and doesn’t change how forecasters are ranked. It’s just easier to interpret.
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: Explains the Brier Index formula and scale: 100% = perfect foresight, 50% = always predicting 50%, 0% = maximally wrong
items.reasoning: Useful methodological explanation for the AI forecasting community.
items.summary_html: Explains the Brier Index formula and scale: 100% = perfect foresight, 50% = always predicting 50%, 0% = maximally wrong
items.content_html:
Brier Index = (1 − √Brier score) × 100%.

The scale:

• 100% = perfect foresight;

• 50% = always predicting 50%;

• 0% = maximally wrong.

A Brier Index of 70% is the score you’d get by predicting 70% on events that happen and 30% on events that don’t.

The Brier Index is a monotonic transformation of the Brier score. So, it’s still strictly proper and doesn’t change how forecasters are ranked. It’s just easier to interpret.

items.title:
What's scary is how easy it is to take down anyone's site if they're hosted on @Hetzner_Online 

I'm...
items.content:
What's scary is how easy it is to take down anyone's site if they're hosted on @Hetzner_Online 

I'm the biggest Hetzner fan btw so that's why I say it

But you can just send an abuse report to any site on Hetzner and if the site owner doesn't reply within 24 hours they generally block the server IP taking down the site

It happened to me this week for the nth time!

The question is if you're a solo biz like me, if you get to that email on time

Essentially you have to be on standby every day of the week forever just to keep your sites up

@Cloudflare is much more reasonable with abuse reports, taking the side of their paying customers first, and only taking down if forced by a court (as it should be!)
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: levelsio warns about how easy it is to take down sites on Hetzner via abuse reports, comparing favorably to Cloudflare's approach of siding with paying customers. Very high engagement (790 likes, 116K views).

It happened to me this week for the nth time!

The question is if you're a solo biz like me, if you get to that email on time

Essentially you have to be on standby every day of the week forever just to keep your sites up

@Cloudflare is much more reasonable with abuse reports, taking the side of their paying customers first, and only taking down if forced by a court (as it should be!)

items.title:
An important caveat: the Brier Index is not a “percentage correct” metric.

A Brier Index of 70% doe...
items.content:
An important caveat: the Brier Index is not a “percentage correct” metric.

A Brier Index of 70% does not mean a forecaster “got 70% of questions right.” It measures how far probability forecasts are from the truth, rescaled to 0–100%.

A forecaster who predicts 51% on events that happen and 49% on events that don’t would get the direction right 100% of the time—but score just 51% on the Brier Index.
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: Caveat about Brier Index not being a 'percentage correct' metric
items.reasoning: Methodological clarification, part of the thread.
items.summary_html: Caveat about Brier Index not being a 'percentage correct' metric

items.title: @rnjnwng I got my job at Microsoft without having a resume. @vicgundotra called me and bought a tabl...
items.content:
@rnjnwng I got my job at Microsoft without having a resume. @vicgundotra called me and bought a tablet PC from me after seeing me talking about it in some online forums and my blog. Later he called back and said he wanted me to come to Microsoft and teach his employees how to do online evangelism. 

While there I did 600 interviews of Microsoft employees, and helped write its first social media policy. 

Willy Wonka is a great film. 

Vic only advertised the job after he offered it to me to cover any bases.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer shares personal career anecdote about getting hired at Microsoft through online presence rather than a resume.

While there I did 600 interviews of Microsoft employees, and helped write its first social media policy.

items.summary_html: Ant Grasso comments on T-Mobile's perspective about connectivity challenges in logistics, noting that autonomous equipment and fleet coordination depend on stable rural coverage.
items.content_html:
@TMobileBusiness Good perspective on a problem that is frequently underestimated in logistics.

T-Mobile highlights an essential point because autonomous equipment, fleet coordination, and remote operations depend on stable connectivity across large territories.

When coverage holds in rural environments, real-time control and visibility become operational tools rather than theoretical capabilities.

@TMobileBusiness

items.title: @roydanroy Great post. As a test question, I sometimes ask something like: suppose in the bound we p...
items.content: @roydanroy Great post. As a test question, I sometimes ask something like: suppose in the bound we penalize “compressible” solutions. Would an incompressible overfitting solution have a good bound? Probably not, since there are many more ways to be incompressible than compressible.

items.content_html: @roydanroy Great post. As a test question, I sometimes ask something like: suppose in the bound we penalize “compressible” solutions. Would an incompressible overfitting solution have a good bound? Probably not, since there are many more ways to be incompressible than compressible.

items.title:
The math of modern warfare.

Iran tested and upgraded these cheap Shahed drones in Ukraine, where af...
items.content:
The math of modern warfare.

Iran tested and upgraded these cheap Shahed drones in Ukraine, where after 4 years of war (and based on the *official* statements), the Ukrainian air defense is capable of neutralizing 80% of these drones while the remaining 20% (plus the Ukrainian own interceptor missiles) hit the targets.

Russia sent to Ukraine about 60,000 drones since 2022.

items.summary: Burkov discusses military drone warfare statistics from Ukraine, noting Iran's Shahed drone capabilities

Iran tested and upgraded these cheap Shahed drones in Ukraine, where after 4 years of war (and based on the *official* statements), the Ukrainian air defense is capable of neutralizing 80% of these drones while the remaining 20% (plus the Ukrainian own interceptor missiles) hit the targets.

Russia sent to Ukraine about 60,000 drones since 2022.

items.title: @elonmusk It's scary that it took so long for white male and white people in general to stop being a...
items.content:
@elonmusk It's scary that it took so long for white male and white people in general to stop being afraid of being bullied for wanting to have the same rights and protection as others. You cannot protect one group of people at the expense of bullying and shaming another one.

The time it took though - decades - is what's wrong.

I remember my friend went shopping with his gf, she completely changed his style:

He arrived fully dressed in brown, brown shirt, brown corduroy rib pants, brown shoes, he looked like a 💩 turd

A week later she dumped him!

items.title: I'm an immigrant in Portugal for the last 5 years, have spent millions € there, hired lots of people...
items.content:
I'm an immigrant in Portugal for the last 5 years, have spent millions € there, hired lots of people here, learnt the langauge (eu falo português), studied and know Portugal's history better than many Portuguese

Yet immigrants who bribed their way into the system, get government welfare, get free government housing, don't speak a single word of Portuguese, now have Portuguese citizenship, yet I don't!

I'm being treated differently for sure, but the opposite way of how I expected to be 😂
